text.title: Tree Testing: Method, Metrics, and Task Design Rules
text.summary: What tree testing is, the four key metrics, tree structure constraints, and task writing rules including the leak-check requirement.

section.title: What It Is
section.p: Tree testing evaluates the findability of topics in a website hierarchy by having participants navigate a text-only version of the site structure (no design, no content snippets, no search) to locate answers to scenario tasks. It isolates IA quality from visual design, content quality, and search — measuring the structure itself.

rule.statement: Use tree testing after card sorting produces a draft IA. It validates the structure; card sorting creates it. Do not skip card sorting and go directly to tree testing on an IA designed by the team without user input.
rule.rationale: Tree testing tells you if users can navigate a given structure — it does not help you discover the right structure. Testing a team-designed IA directly produces a verdict without the prior user-input that would make iteration actionable.

rule.statement: Track four metrics per task: success rate (found correct answer — target >80%), directness (found it without backtracking — target >70%), first-click correctness (started in the right section), and time to complete.
rule.rationale: Success rate alone is insufficient. A task with 80% success but 50% first-click-correct means users found it eventually but the IA still feels wrong — a different fix than a task with poor success and poor first-click. First click predicts 87% success when correct vs only 46% when wrong.

rule.statement: Cap the tree at 3-4 levels and ~100 nodes. Use actual navigation labels — no placeholder text. Match the planned structure exactly.
rule.rationale: Trees deeper than 4 levels or larger than 100 nodes fatigue participants and inflate failure rates artificially. Placeholder text prevents testing the actual label vocabulary that drives real navigation behavior.

rule.statement: Write tasks as scenarios, not as navigation instructions. The task must not contain the destination label or any word-stem overlapping the target node. Run a leak-check pass on every task before launch.
rule.rationale: Tasks that echo the destination label (e.g., "Find the Returns page" when the label is "Returns") test label recognition, not information architecture. Leak-check is mandatory — even experienced researchers fail this regularly, and LLM-generated tasks almost always fail it.

example.note: Task scenario that does not reveal the destination label "Returns".
example.code: "You want to send back a product you ordered. Where would you go?"

example.note: Task that reveals the destination label directly.
example.code: "Find Returns" — gives away the label "Navigate to the support section" — too directive
